output:
# CADENCE: Closing the MYOPIA Gap in LLM Safety -- A Trajectory-Aware Drift Accumulator that Detects Multi-Turn Escalation while Exactly Collapsing to Per-Turn Moderation

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

The deployed defense in nearly every shipped LLM system is stateless, per-turn moderation: a content classifier that scores each user message and/or each model response in isolation and returns a per-turn harm scalar. This class is ubiquitous precisely because it is cheap, auditable, and memoryless -- Llama Guard [10], the OpenAI moderation endpoint [11], ShieldGemma [12], WildGuard [13], and character-level toxicity models such as Perspective API [14] all operate this way, and single-prompt jailbreak defenses such as perplexity filters [15, 16] and smoothing [17] likewise inspect one input at a time. Against single-shot attacks this regime works as intended: optimization-based suffixes (GCG/AdvBench) [2] and single-prompt textual jailbreaks such as Do Anything Now (DAN) [3] place identifiable malicious content into one input, so once the attack is known the per-turn classifier can flag it. The same per-turn assumption underlies most published attack-and-judge loops, including PAIR [4] and TAP [5], which still concentrate adversarial pressure into individual prompts.

Crescendo [1] breaks this regime by construction. It begins with a benign abstract question and escalates in small, individually benign steps, so the harmful target is reached only through a sequence in which no single turn carries identifiable malicious content; input filters therefore defend poorly even after the attack is discovered. Successor attacks and human red-team studies confirm this is a general recipe rather than a single trick: ActorAttack / Derail-Yourself [7], Chain-of-Attack [8], Multi-turn Human Jailbreaks (MHJ) [9], and persuasion-based jailbreaks (PAP) [6] all distribute harm across turns. The crucial point is that the limitation is REPRESENTATIONAL, not a matter of calibration: because the attack's structure is orthogonal to per-turn scoring, no threshold on a per-turn classifier can catch an escalation engineered to keep every turn below it. Strengthening or re-tuning a single-turn classifier cannot close a gap whose harm exists only in the cumulative trajectory.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

We name the structural limitation MYOPIA (Moderation Yielding Only Per-Input Assessment). Deployed moderation emits a per-turn harm SCALAR -- a function of one message -- while Crescendo's harm is a property of the TRAJECTORY: the conversation's cumulative, monotone semantic drift toward a harmful target region, integrated over turns. A per-turn scalar is STRUCTURALLY INCAPABLE of expressing a cross-turn accumulation (a path / sequence-function property); this is a representational impossibility, not an under-tuned classifier. The blindness scales predictably: evasion is easiest when escalation is spread over many turns each kept below the per-turn threshold, so the per-turn detector's miss-rate grows with the number of benign escalation steps N and with the smallness of per-step drift delta -- detection probability tends to zero as delta shrinks and N grows. Symmetrically, the gap VANISHES IFF the attack is single-turn, or at least one turn is independently detectable (carries identifiable malicious content above the per-turn threshold).

The upgrade is CADENCE (Cumulative Accumulation of Drift for Escalation-aware Negotiation of Conversational Escalation): a musical cadence resolves a sequence, and CADENCE resolves the trajectory that Crescendo builds. CADENCE replaces the per-turn scalar with a bounded, online accumulator. It embeds each turn into an intent/harm space, computes the marginal drift toward a latent harmful-target direction this turn, accumulates it across turns with a decaying CUSUM/EWMA-style recursion over O(1) state [29, 30], and intervenes when the running statistic crosses a threshold tau EVEN IF the current turn is individually benign. The central guarantee is an EXACT COLLAPSE: under the memoryless default (decay lambda = 0, slack k = 0, drift d(t) set to the deployed per-turn score c(t), tau = the baseline threshold), CADENCE reduces decision-for-decision to the deployed per-turn classifier. It is therefore a strict, safe generalization -- turning on cross-turn accumulation can only add detections the baseline misses, with provably zero single-turn regression. This work is a DEFENSE, never an attack: it detects and blocks gradual escalation and produces no harmful artifacts of any kind.

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can we replace the deployed per-turn safety SCALAR with a trajectory-aware drift ACCUMULATOR that detects and blocks cumulative multi-turn escalation toward a harmful target -- which per-turn moderation is representationally incapable of catching -- while exactly collapsing to the deployed per-turn classifier under a memoryless default so that single-turn behavior never regresses and benign deep-dive conversations are not over-blocked?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

(P1) Per-turn moderation is the dominant production safety pattern. The deployed defense in nearly every shipped LLM system is a stateless content classifier that scores each user message and/or model response in isolation and returns a per-turn harm scalar [10, 11, 12, 13, 14]. This class is cheap, auditable, and ubiquitous precisely because it is memoryless. Multi-turn jailbreaks such as Crescendo [1] are constructed so that every individual turn is independently benign, with the harmful target reached only through a sequence of small benign-looking steps; successors and human red-team studies confirm this is a general recipe, not a single trick [7, 8, 9, 6]. Because the attack's structure is orthogonal to per-turn scoring, it defeats the entire defense class by construction. This is a broad latent vulnerability sitting under the most widely deployed guardrail, not a corner case.

(P2) The failure mode is a SILENT safety failure, which is the most dangerous kind. Under Crescendo, every turn passes moderation, so all per-turn safety dashboards -- false-negative rate, blocked-message rate, per-message harm scores -- look healthy, while the conversation as a whole is fully jailbroken. The harm is invisible to exactly the instruments operators trust, and it concentrates where guardrails are believed to hold rather than at obvious adversarial inputs (cf. the single-prompt regime of [2, 4, 5]). An operator can be 100 percent compliant on per-turn metrics and 0 percent safe on the trajectory. Closing this gap is high-leverage because it removes a blind spot that current telemetry cannot even surface.

(P3) Our defense is deployable rather than merely diagnostic. CADENCE is engineered so that under its memoryless default (lambda = 0, k = 0, drift d(t) set to the deployed per-turn score, tau the deployed threshold) it reproduces the existing classifier decision-for-decision -- an EXACT-COLLAPSE guarantee. This makes it a strict, safe superset of current moderation: turning on cross-turn accumulation can only add detections that the per-turn baseline misses, with provably zero single-turn regression and no new over-refusal on benign single prompts [24, 25]. Practically, a team can ship CADENCE in shadow mode behind their existing Llama-Guard-style classifier and dial up the accumulator, which is the difference between an analysis paper and an adoptable contribution.

(P4) The trajectory-accumulator lens generalizes well beyond jailbreaks. Any threat whose signature is gradual, cross-turn drift toward a latent target -- persuasion and social engineering [6], slow data exfiltration, or agentic tool-use drift where each tool call is locally reasonable -- is invisible to stateless filters but visible to a bounded accumulator over a harm-target direction. Framing conversational safety as integrating a path property rather than thresholding isolated points shifts the field from stateless content filtering toward stateful conversational safety, and gives a single mechanism that covers a family of slow-manipulation attacks.

(P5) Finally, the formulation is auditable and quantitative. CADENCE predicts that detection lift over the per-turn baseline should scale with (number of escalation steps) x (per-step semantic drift) x (monotonicity of the trajectory toward the harmful target). This converts the vague intuition "multi-turn is dangerous" into a measurable quantity: the same harmful payload spread over more turns with smaller per-step drift is exactly where per-turn miss-rate is highest and where the accumulator's advantage is largest, and the two effects can be plotted against each other. That gives reviewers and operators a falsifiable, instrumented account of when and why the defense helps -- and, symmetrically, the precise conditions (single-turn attack, or some turn independently detectable) under which the gap vanishes.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

Obstacle 1: ESTIMATING THE LATENT HARMFUL TARGET WITHOUT KNOWING IT (BINDING). This is the binding constraint: every other obstacle is downstream of getting the harm-direction geometry right. A Crescendo conversation drifts toward SOME harmful goal, but the defender does not know which one, and the space of harmful goals is large and open-ended (synthesis of a weapon, malware, disinformation, self-harm instructions). The naive fix -- "measure cosine distance from the current turn to a fixed harmful reference prompt" -- fails three ways: (a) it presupposes the target, so it misses any conversation drifting toward a goal not in the reference set; (b) surface text of each turn is benign by construction, so raw turn embeddings carry little harm signal; (c) a single fixed reference cannot cover a taxonomy of hazards. The principled requirement is IDENTIFIABILITY of a harm direction that the MODEL ITSELF treats as harmful -- the direction in activation/embedding space such that moving along it is what the model would refuse if asked directly. Two concrete realizations: (1) read the refusal direction from activations [21] or a representation-engineering harm reading [22] on open models with weight access; (2) for black-box models, project onto the nearest centroid over a hazard taxonomy built from harmful-behavior corpora [23]. The drift signal d(t) must then measure marginal movement toward this harmful manifold, computed online and per-turn-cheap. If the harm direction is mis-estimated, the accumulator integrates noise and either over-blocks or never fires -- so this obstacle gates the whole method.

Obstacle 2: SEPARATING MALICIOUS MONOTONE ESCALATION FROM BENIGN DEEP-DIVE. Legitimate users routinely move toward sensitive regions: a security researcher discussing exploit classes, a clinician discussing overdose thresholds, a historian discussing atrocities, a journalist verifying extremist claims. These trajectories approach the harmful manifold but differ structurally from an attack: they are NON-MONOTONE (they circle, qualify, retreat, cite context) and topically justified, whereas Crescendo is engineered MONOTONE escalation toward a single target. A naive accumulator that integrates any approach to sensitive content over-blocks these users -- a real over-refusal harm that erodes utility and trust. The decay term lambda and the slack term k are exactly the knobs that encode this distinction: lambda lets benign, slow, or non-monotone drift dissipate between turns, and k absorbs per-turn drift that stays below an escalation floor, while sustained monotone drift above k accumulates past tau. This is an intrinsic precision/recall tradeoff and CANNOT be assumed -- it must be validated on benign-but-sensitive MULTI-TURN data (XSTest [24], OR-Bench [25] extended to multi-turn), not just on clearly-benign chat. If lambda and k cannot be set to pass benign deep-dives while catching escalation, the method is not deployable.

Obstacle 3: ADAPTIVE ATTACKER WHO KNOWS THE MONITOR. The deepest robustness test. An attacker who knows CADENCE exists and knows its form can: (a) PACE escalation so per-step drift d(t) stays just below the slack k, never triggering accumulation; (b) inject benign "cool-down" turns to exploit the decay lambda and bleed the accumulator back down; (c) take TOPICAL DETOURS that approach the target obliquely. A static tau alone is insufficient against this. The honest claim is NOT "CADENCE cannot be evaded"; it is that CADENCE converts a previously FREE attack into one with measurable COST. A per-turn filter fails SILENTLY against Crescendo -- the attacker pays nothing. Under CADENCE, evasion requires more turns, lower per-step drift, and longer conversations, all of which are observable and quantifiable as an attacker-cost curve. The method should degrade GRACEFULLY (cost rises smoothly as the attacker slows) rather than exhibit a cliff the attacker can step around. Robustness here likely needs randomized or expected-cost analysis (e.g., randomized tau or k, or jitter on the drift floor) so the attacker cannot solve for an exact safe pacing. Naive static-threshold analysis overstates security.

Obstacle 4: THE COLLAPSE-VS-EXPRESSIVENESS TENSION. CADENCE must be expressive enough to detect gradual cross-turn escalation, yet must reduce EXACTLY to the deployed per-turn classifier at its memoryless default (lambda = 0, k = 0, d(t) = c(t), tau = baseline threshold) -- this exact collapse (C4) is the strict-generalization guarantee and is enforced as a build-gating unit test. The tension is real: a richly learned drift model or a learned accumulator that CANNOT be driven to reproduce the per-turn decision decision-for-decision breaks the guarantee and reintroduces single-turn regression risk. Many natural design choices (a neural sequence model over turns, a learned attention pooling) are expressive but have no exact memoryless reduction. The constraint "expressive enough to add trajectory sensitivity, but provably collapsing to the baseline at the default" rules out otherwise-attractive architectures and forces the drift+accumulator to be an ADDITIVE, identity-defaulting layer on top of the untouched per-turn classifier. Designing for both properties simultaneously is a nontrivial constraint, not a free lunch.

Obstacle 5: BIASED / SCARCE EVALUATION DATA. The empirical foundation is thin and skewed. Multi-turn jailbreak corpora are small and human-crafted [9, 7]; benign-multi-turn-on-sensitive-topics data barely exists as a labeled resource; and the harmful-target labels are themselves model- and policy-dependent (what counts as the harmful manifold depends on the wrapped model's own refusal behavior). Reporting Recall on a handful of Crescendo transcripts overfits to a few attack templates and inflates results. Mitigations: a SAFELY-HELD synthetic escalation generator (produces escalation TRAJECTORIES and scoring signals, never releases harmful payloads), held-out attack templates for transfer evaluation, harmful targets drawn from HarmBench [23] and AdvBench [2], and -- critically -- false-positive rate measured on LARGE benign multi-turn corpora [26, 27, 28] rather than asserted. Without this, any headline number is unfalsifiable.

Why it is still tractable. Each design axis IDENTITY-DEFAULTS to the deployed classifier, so the method is a strict, testable generalization with zero single-turn regression by construction (C4). The harm-direction probe [21, 2] converts the unobservable latent target (Obstacle 1) into a MEASURABLE projection computable per turn. The accumulator S(t) = max(0, lambda*S(t-1) + (d(t) - k)) is O(1) state and online (CUSUM/EWMA form [29]), so it adds millisecond-scale overhead. The collapse identity is a concrete CI build-gating unit test (E_collapse = 0), not a hope. And adaptive robustness (Obstacle 3) is reframed from "prove unbreakable" to "quantify attacker cost," which is achievable with a cost curve. The binding obstacle (1) has at least two independent realizations (activation-space refusal direction for open models; taxonomy-centroid distance for black-box), so the method is not single-point-of-failure.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The wedge has four clauses that must hold SIMULTANEOUSLY in one mechanism: C1 trajectory / full-conversation level; C2 cumulative drift toward a latent harmful-target direction (a path property); C3 online, streaming, bounded O(1) state, cheap per turn; C4 EXACT collapse to the deployed per-turn classifier under a memoryless default. Every prior-art class covers at most two of the four, so the combination has never been occupied.

NEAR-MISS TABLE

| Prior-art class | Representative work | C1 traj-level | C2 harm-target drift | C3 online/bounded | C4 exact-collapse | Clauses missed |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Per-turn input/output guardrails | Llama Guard [10], OpenAI moderation [11], ShieldGemma [12], WildGuard [13], Perspective API [14] | No | No | Yes | Trivially the target | MISS C1, C2 |
| Single-prompt jailbreak / smoothing defenses | Perplexity filter [15, 16], SmoothLLM [17] | No | No | Yes | No | MISS C1, C2, C4 |
| Full-transcript LLM-as-judge re-scoring each turn | HarmBench-style judges [23], PAIR/TAP judge loops [4, 5] | Yes | Yes | No (heavy, not bounded) | No | MISS C3, C4 |
| Alignment in the weights (as a detector) | RLHF [18, 19], Constitutional AI [20] | Partial (implicit) | Partial (implicit) | No (not a separable monitor) | No | MISS C3, C4 |
| Generic anomaly / changepoint detection | CUSUM [29], EWMA [30], seq. changepoint [31] | Partial | No (no harm-target geometry) | Yes | No | MISS C1*, C2, C4 |
| Multi-turn ATTACK papers | Crescendo [1], ActorAttack [7], Chain-of-Attack [8], MHJ [9], PAP [6] | n/a (attacks) | n/a | n/a | n/a | Cover none (attacks, not defenses) |

(*Changepoint methods can be sequence-level in the abstract, but they detect generic distributional shift, not motion along a latent harmful-target direction, so they fail C1's harm geometry as well as C2 and C4.)

DEEPEST BARRIER. The reason this remained unsolved is that the problem lives in a cell no existing tool was built to occupy. Cross-turn escalation is a SEQUENCE / PATH property: a per-turn classifier is representationally incapable of expressing a cumulative cross-turn accumulation, because its output is a scalar function of one message -- this is a structural impossibility, not an under-tuning that a stronger single-turn classifier could fix. The tools that ARE set-aware -- full-transcript LLM-as-judge re-scoring -- do see the whole conversation (C1, C2) but run at the wrong cost regime (re-reading the growing transcript every turn, unbounded state) and offer no guarantee tying them back to the deployed classifier (miss C3, C4). The tools that ARE cheap and online -- CUSUM/EWMA changepoint detection -- accumulate over time (C2-like, C3) but detect generic distribution change and never encode a harmful-target geometry, nor collapse to a deployed safety classifier (miss C1's harm geometry and C4). Alignment in the weights is exactly what Crescendo defeats and is not a separable, auditable monitor at all. The unclaimed cell is a single bounded online monitor that is simultaneously trajectory-level (C1), harm-target-drift-aware (C2), cheap and streaming with O(1) state (C3), AND exactly collapses to deployed per-turn moderation under its memoryless default (C4). That cell is CADENCE.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

METHOD detail.
- Per-turn embedding e(t). Open models (weight access): read the refusal direction [21] or a RepE harm reading [22] from hidden activations at a chosen layer for the turn (and the model's draft response); e(t) is the activation, the harm direction h is the probe vector. Black-box models (API only): e(t) is a sentence embedding of the user turn concatenated with the model's own response, and h is the direction to the nearest centroid over a hazard taxonomy built from HarmBench behaviors [23].
- Marginal drift d(t). The projection of the per-turn STEP onto the harm direction: d(t) = max(0, <e(t) - e(t-1), h>) for the activation path, or the increase in proximity to the nearest hazard centroid for the black-box path. Clamped >= 0 so only movement TOWARD harm accumulates; retreats do not create negative credit beyond the decay.
- Accumulator. S(t) = max(0, lambda*S(t-1) + (d(t) - k)), a bounded, one-state CUSUM/EWMA recursion [29]. lambda in [0,1] is decay; k >= 0 is the per-step escalation slack.
- Intervention policy at tau. When S(t) >= tau, apply one of: block the turn, insert a safe-completion, route to a human reviewer, or reset/trim conversation context to bleed the accumulator. The DEPLOYED per-turn classifier c(t) runs IN PARALLEL and unchanged, so any individually-harmful turn is caught immediately regardless of S(t).

B-LADDER ablation (each rung capacity-matched).
- B1: lambda = 0, k = 0, d(t) = c(t), tau = baseline threshold -> EXACT per-turn baseline (the collapse point).
- B2: B1 + harm-direction drift d(t) replacing c(t) as the signal, still memoryless (lambda = 0) -> isolates the value of the trajectory-aware drift signal.
- B3: B2 + accumulation (lambda > 0, k > 0) -> isolates the value of cross-turn integration.
- B4: B3 + adaptive/randomized thresholding (jittered tau or k) -> isolates adaptive-attacker robustness.
Capacity-matched control at each rung: a parameter-count-matched model WITHOUT the trajectory structure (e.g., same extra parameters fed only the current turn) so any gain attributes to trajectory structure, not added capacity.

DATASETS (named).
- Attack / eval: Crescendo transcripts [1]; a safely-held synthetic escalation generator (trajectories and signals only, no harmful payloads); Multi-turn Human Jailbreaks / MHJ [9]; ActorAttack / Derail-Yourself [7]; HarmBench behaviors as harmful targets [23]; AdvBench [2].
- Benign multi-turn (for FPR): MT-Bench [26]; WildChat [27]; LMSYS-Chat-1M [28].
- Over-refusal / benign-sensitive: XSTest [24]; OR-Bench [25], both extended to multi-turn.
- Target models to wrap: open Llama-2 / Llama-3, Vicuna, Mistral (activation access for the probe path) and a black-box API model (embedding-only path).

METRICS (primary first).
- HEADLINE: attack detection/prevention rate at a FIXED benign false-positive rate (FPR <= 1% on benign multi-turn).
- ROC-AUC over whole conversations.
- TURN-TO-DETECTION (earliness): number of turns before the harmful target is reached.
- EXACT-COLLAPSE match rate on single-turn benchmarks: E_collapse = fraction of decisions disagreeing with the baseline, target 0.
- Per-turn latency and state overhead: must be O(1) state and ms-scale.
- ADAPTIVE-ATTACKER cost curve: turns/queries required as a function of the enforced per-step drift cap epsilon.
- Transfer: across wrapped models and across unseen attack templates.
- Over-refusal rate on XSTest [24] / OR-Bench [25].

BASELINES.
- Per-turn Llama Guard on each message [10].
- Per-turn classifier + running MAX (naive memory, NOT accumulation) -- shows accumulation beats max.
- Sliding-window concatenation fed to the per-turn classifier.
- Full-transcript LLM-as-judge re-scored every turn (the expensive C1/C2 oracle) [23] -- CADENCE should approach its detection at far lower cost.
- Perplexity filter [15].
- No defense.

COLLAPSE UNIT TEST (CI build gate). Instantiate CADENCE in collapse mode (lambda = 0, k = 0, d(t) = c(t), tau = baseline threshold) and assert decision-for-decision equality to the deployed per-turn classifier across a large single-turn suite: AdvBench harmful [2] + benign + XSTest [24]. Adversarial edges: empty history, first turn of a conversation, a one-turn conversation, repeated identical turns. Assert E_collapse <= 1e-6; any disagreement FAILS the build.

EXPECTED OUTCOMES (hedged, falsifiable).
- B1 reproduces the deployed baseline exactly (E_collapse = 0).
- On gradual multi-turn attacks, CADENCE detects a large fraction that per-turn moderation misses, at matched low benign FPR, and detects EARLIER as escalation proceeds (lower turn-to-detection).
- On single-turn attacks and on benign conversations, CADENCE matches the baseline: no detection regression, no extra over-blocking above baseline FPR.
- Against an adaptive attacker, escalation is forced to be slower and longer (a MEASURED cost), not blocked outright.
- NULL FALSIFIER: if CADENCE gives no detection lift over per-turn moderation at matched FPR on gradual multi-turn attacks, then MYOPIA is not the binding constraint and the hypothesis is REFUTED.

FALSIFIABILITY TRIAD.
1. Collapse test passes: memoryless CADENCE == per-turn classifier exactly (E_collapse <= 1e-6).
2. Detection lift LOCALIZES: present on gradual multi-turn attacks; VANISHES on single-turn attacks (already caught per-turn) and on benign multi-turn (no false trigger above baseline FPR).
3. Under an enforced per-step drift cap epsilon, attacker cost (turns to reach harm) rises measurably and MONOTONICALLY as epsilon shrinks -- a smooth cost curve, not a cliff.

Alignment-in-the-weights defenses (RLHF [18, 19], Constitutional AI [20]) are complementary but out of scope as a separable monitor: they are precisely what Crescendo defeats, and EWMA / sequential changepoint theory [30, 31] supplies the accumulator's statistical lineage without supplying the harm-target geometry CADENCE adds.

### Responsible-AI folds (advisory)

VERDICT: YELLOW (advisory). The artifact is defense-framed, contains no operational harmful content or attack payloads, and reads as a detector, not an attack recipe. Work proceeds; the folds below should be inserted into the design and evaluation plan.

1. DUAL-USE / INFOHAZARD SAFEGUARDS. Constrain the synthetic escalation generator to emit ONLY drift trajectories and scoring signals (per-turn d(t), accumulator S(t)), never harmful payloads or completable attack text, and gate this with a unit test asserting zero harmful-string emission. Access-control all real attack transcripts used in eval (Crescendo [1], MHJ [9], ActorAttack [7]) and do not release them; disclose any newly discovered escalation paths only under coordinated responsible disclosure to the wrapped-model owners.

2. OVER-REFUSAL AS A PRIMARY METRIC WITH A BOUNDED BUDGET. Promote false-positive rate on benign-but-sensitive MULTI-TURN data (XSTest [24] and OR-Bench [25] extended to multi-turn) to a co-headline metric reported alongside detection rate, not a secondary line. Pre-register a bounded over-refusal budget (e.g., multi-turn benign-sensitive FPR <= 1%) and treat any lambda/k/tau setting that exceeds it as a failed, non-deployable configuration.

3. USER-VISIBLE INTERVENTION AND CONTESTABILITY. On any S(t) >= tau intervention, surface a user-visible reason and provide an appeal/override path rather than silently blocking or trimming context; log the accumulator state and threshold so each intervention is auditable and reversible.

4. FAIRNESS / DISPARATE IMPACT. Stratify BOTH false-positive rate and detection rate by language, dialect/sociolect, and topic domain (e.g., self-harm-for-support, LGBTQ+, religious, minority-language, and security-research content); audit the harm-direction probe h for protected-attribute proxying and flag any stratum whose benign FPR is an outlier (e.g., > 2x the pooled benign FPR).

5. PRIVACY / DATA MINIMIZATION. Process minimal accumulator state (O(1)) and avoid long-term storage of raw transcripts beyond what detection requires; document a retention policy and ensure monitoring state is auditable and deletable.

6. CLAIMS AS HYPOTHESES TO VALIDATE. State adaptive-attacker robustness (Obstacle 3), cross-model and cross-template generalization, the P4 "beyond jailbreaks" claims (persuasion, slow exfiltration, agentic tool-use drift), and fairness parity as HYPOTHESES tied to their named falsifiers and metrics, not as demonstrated results.

7. POLICY DEPENDENCE OF THE HARM MANIFOLD. Document explicitly that "harmful" is defined by the wrapped model's own refusal behavior/policy (the harm direction h is read from that model), so the harm manifold is model- and policy-relative; make whose-policy-applies explicit, contestable, and updatable, and re-validate lambda/k/tau whenever the underlying policy changes.

## 4. References and Evidence

1. Russinovich, M., Salem, A., Eldan, R. "Great, Now Write an Article About That: The Crescendo Multi-Turn LLM Jailbreak Attack." USENIX Security Symposium, 2025 (preprint 2024).
2. Zou, A., Wang, Z., Carlini, N., Nasr, M., Kolter, J. Z., Fredrikson, M. "Universal and Transferable Adversarial Attacks on Aligned Language Models." arXiv, 2023. (GCG; AdvBench).
3. Shen, X., Chen, Z., Backes, M., Shen, Y., Zhang, Y. "Do Anything Now: Characterizing and Evaluating In-The-Wild Jailbreak Prompts on Large Language Models." ACM CCS, 2024.
4. Chao, P., Robey, A., Dobriban, E., Hassani, H., Pappas, G. J., Wong, E. "Jailbreaking Black Box Large Language Models in Twenty Queries." arXiv, 2023. (PAIR).
5. Mehrotra, A., Zampetakis, M., Kassianik, P., Nelson, B., Anderson, H., Singer, Y., Karbasi, A. "Tree of Attacks: Jailbreaking Black-Box LLMs Automatically." NeurIPS, 2024. (TAP).
6. Zeng, Y., Lin, H., Zhang, J., Yang, D., Jia, R., Shi, W. "How Johnny Can Persuade LLMs to Jailbreak Them: Rethinking Persuasion to Challenge AI Safety by Humanizing LLMs." ACL, 2024. (PAP).
7. Ren, Q., et al. "Derail Yourself: Multi-turn LLM Jailbreak Attack through Self-discovered Clues." arXiv, 2024. (ActorAttack).
8. Yang, X., Tang, X., Hu, S., Han, J. "Chain of Attack: A Semantic-Driven Contextual Multi-Turn Attacker for LLM." arXiv, 2024.
9. Li, N., et al. "LLM Defenses Are Not Robust to Multi-Turn Human Jailbreaks Yet." arXiv, 2024. (MHJ).
10. Inan, H., et al. "Llama Guard: LLM-based Input-Output Safeguard for Human-AI Conversations." arXiv, 2023.
11. Markov, T., et al. "A Holistic Approach to Undesired Content Detection in the Real World." AAAI, 2023. (OpenAI moderation).
12. Zeng, W., et al. "ShieldGemma: Generative AI Content Moderation Based on Gemma." arXiv, 2024.
13. Han, S., et al. "WildGuard: Open One-Stop Moderation Tools for Safety Risks, Jailbreaks, and Refusals of LLMs." NeurIPS, 2024.
14. Lees, A., et al. "A New Generation of Perspective API: Efficient Multilingual Character-level Transformers." KDD, 2022.
15. Alon, G., Kamfonas, M. "Detecting Language Model Attacks with Perplexity." arXiv, 2023.
16. Jain, N., et al. "Baseline Defenses for Adversarial Attacks Against Aligned Language Models." arXiv, 2023.
17. Robey, A., Wong, E., Hassani, H., Pappas, G. J. "SmoothLLM: Defending Large Language Models Against Jailbreaking Attacks." arXiv, 2023.
18. Ouyang, L., et al. "Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback." NeurIPS, 2022. (InstructGPT; RLHF).
19. Bai, Y., et al. "Training a Helpful and Harmless Assistant with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback." arXiv, 2022. (HH-RLHF).
20. Bai, Y., et al. "Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback." arXiv, 2022.
